justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Latency histogram gap explicitly identified as blocking SLA monitoring — called out as 'the single biggest operational gap' and escalated with the analogy 'shipping with no speedometer and a speed limit'. (2) Undifferentiated failure counter clearly identified — explained that on-call cannot distinguish S3 errors from validation failures, with MTTR impact noted. (3) Specific recommendations provided: upload_duration_seconds histogram and error_type label (s3|validation|timeout) on the failure counter. (4) File size tracking correctly classified as NICE-TO-HAVE, not a blocker. (5) Verdict is NOT READY with clear must-fix items in escalations.

observations.text: Latency histogram gap tied directly to SLA unmonitorability with clear escalation
Failure counter issue explained with concrete on-call scenario (3am spike, can't distinguish S3 vs bad files)
Clean separation of MUST-HAVE vs NICE-TO-HAVE in metrics gaps
File size distribution metric correctly marked as NICE-TO-HAVE for capacity planning
Went beyond rubric expectations with thorough resilience analysis (memory buffering, connection exhaustion, idempotency)
Escalations are well-structured with clear reasoning for why each is blocking
In-flight request gauge recommendation is reasonable but not in the rubric — no penalty, just extra scope

agent_output_excerpt: {"integration_assessment":{"component_interactions":[{"from":"client","to":"upload_service","status":"correct","issue":"Accepts uploads up to 50MB with format validation"},{"from":"upload_service","to":"s3","status":"risk","issue":"S3 failures are not broken out from general request failures. When S3 has a regional degradation, on-call cannot distinguish S3-specific errors from validation failures or other request errors without grepping logs. This delays incident response."},{"from":"upload_ser
